title: Political promises: One Nation's cost-of-living policies may top $90b
full_text:
One Nation's signature cost-of-living policies could cost the federal budget more than $90 billion, with economists questioning whether Pauline Hanson's proposed spending cuts would even come close to paying for her party's growing suite of tax breaks and new spending.
The right-wing party has promised to halve the fuel excise for three years, introduce income splitting for couples with dependants, remove GST on insurance policies, eliminate excise on alcohol sold in bars, boost Medicare funding, let pensioners earn more without losing welfare and raise the tax-free threshold to $35,000 for retirees.
One Nation said before last year's federal election it would pay for the package with a suite of cuts including slashing spending on climate change, means-testing the NDIS and abolishing the Education Department, but economists say some of the party's proposed savings appear dubious.
"At face value, at least, they are claiming to more than pay for all their goodies ... but I would be pretty sceptical that the measures they have identified will produce the savings they claim," said independent economist Saul Eslake.
The party has not released official Parliamentary Budget Office estimates for the cost of all of its policies, but a review by AFR Weekend of publicly available estimates suggests its cost-of-living package could cost the budget at least $90 billion over the four-year forward estimates.
One Nation's pledge to halve the fuel excise for three years could cost about $30 billion, based on the $2.55 billion cost of the Albanese government's three-month cut after oil prices spiked during the US-Iran conflict.
Income splitting for couples with dependant children could cost a further $32 billion over four years, based on One Nation senator Malcolm Roberts' previous estimate of $8 billion a year, provided in a speech to the Senate in March last year. Roberts also told the Senate that removing GST from insurance policies and excise from alcohol sold in bars would collectively cost $4 billion per year, while One Nation has promised to increase Medicare funding by $3 billion annually to combat fraud.
As part of its cost-of-living package, One Nation also promised to raise the tax-free threshold to $35,000 for self-funded retirees over 67 who received no government support and also promised to allow aged and veteran service pensioners to work without any effect on their welfare payments. The cost of these policies is unclear.
Opposition Leader Angus Taylor warned in a speech on Thursday that One Nation's policies would smash the budget bottom line, force interest rates up 3 percentage points, almost triple the nation's $1 trillion debt load and potentially spark a sovereign debt crisis.
Hanson hit back at Taylor in a video on Friday, urging the Liberal leader to "stop denigrating One Nation".
"I'm very disappointed with this, you know, Angus, I'm not your enemy," Hanson said.
The party outlined a savings package before the last election worth $90 billion per year. But Eslake said One Nation had made some "silly mistakes", while other savings measures were "dubious". These included saving $3 billion a year by eliminating federal spending on diversity, equity and inclusion (DEI) programs, as well as $1 billion by cutting funding to the World Bank and the World Economic Forum.
Former Finance Department deputy secretary Stephen Bartos said the government contributed only about $200 million a year to the World Bank and made no formal contribution to the World Economic Forum - a privately run think tank - meaning One Nation's figure was probably an overestimate.
A One Nation spokesman said some of the party's 2025 election policies were costed by the PBO, while others were costed internally by looking at the federal budget papers. He said the costings would be updated in due course.
Hanson said last month the party was unable to cost its policies with the PBO ahead of the 2025 election because One Nation was not officially considered a parliamentary party, though the budget watchdog's website says the service can be used by all parliamentarians.
Eslake said One Nation's proposal to save $35 billion per year by abolishing the Department of Climate Change and the schemes it administered would probably lead to higher power prices and more frequent blackouts.
"A lot of what the government is spending in that space is inducing investment in additional generation and transmission infrastructure. If they stop spending it, then that investment would stop," Eslake said.
Eslake said he was unsure why One Nation wanted to abolish the Australian Curriculum, Assessment and Reporting Authority and NAPLAN given they provided useful information on whether children were learning, while the $12.5 billion in claimed savings from abolishing programs for Indigenous Australians was probably an overestimate.
Bartos said abolishing the Education Department was an odd proposal unless One Nation was also proposing to end federal funding to schools.
"If they're proposing to keep transferring money to the states for education under the national agreements, somebody's got to do that. So, if they've abolished the Education Department, who else will take on that?" Bartos said.
One Nation's website says it would conduct a review of the department to eliminate duplication with the states.
Those commitments come on top of its pledge to increase defence spending to 5 per cent of GDP, including 3.5 per cent on core defence and 1.5 per cent on related infrastructure. The PBO estimates increasing defence spending to 3.5 per cent of GDP would cost an extra $225 billion over the next decade.
Fairfax Media Management Pty Limited
author: Michael Read
heading: General Insurance
